config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but it should not automatically outrank individual rights, because that can easily be abused by governments or majorities. In my view, a just society protects basic rights first and then balances them with the common good when necessary. If individual rights are weak, society can become orderly on the surface but unfair in practice.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement as written because in the United States we tend to treat individual rights as the foundation, not something society can override whenever it wants. A society can have noble goals, but if it can simply set aside rights for the "greater good," that opens the door to abuse by governments, majorities, or loud interest groups. That said, rights are not absolute in every situation, since public safety and laws against harm matter, but the burden should be on society to justify any limit very carefully. In my view, a healthy society protects individual liberty first and then limits it only when there is a clear, narrow, and necessary reason.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE: from my perspective in Indonesia, society is important, but it should not get automatic priority over individual rights. I have seen how appeals to "the common good" can be used to pressure minorities, silence criticism, or justify unfair treatment, so I do not trust collective interests to override rights by default. At the same time, I do not believe rights are absolute in every case; public safety, disaster response, and preventing real harm can justify limits, but those limits must be narrow and accountable. A good society is not one that sacrifices the individual easily, but one that can protect the public without crushing personal dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because in the United States we generally treat individual rights as the starting point, not something society gets to outrank whenever it is convenient. I do think society has real claims, especially around safety, public health, and preventing harm, but history shows that "the greater good" can become a very slippery excuse for abuse if it is not tightly limited. The strongest societies are not the ones that demand the most sacrifice from individuals; they are the ones that can protect the common good while still respecting liberty, due process, and minority rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how language about harmony, order, or the common good can be used to pressure minorities and silence criticism, so I do not trust collective interests to outrank rights by default. That said, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every situation; public safety, disaster response, and preventing clear harm can justify limits, but only with narrow rules and real accountability. A healthy society protects the public without treating the individual as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because in the United States I would rather start from the assumption that individual rights come first and the government has to justify any exception. When society gets automatic priority, it becomes too easy to excuse censorship, discrimination, or heavy-handed policy in the name of the “greater good,” and Americans have plenty of historical reasons to be suspicious of that. I do think there are real cases where public safety or preventing harm should limit individual freedom, but those limits should be narrow, temporary, and accountable. A strong society does not need to crush individual rights to function; it earns legitimacy by protecting both the common good and personal liberty.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
